title: LRU Cache
description: Implement LRU Cache using DLL + hashmap

examples.input: cache = LRUCache(2); cache.put(1, 1); cache.put(2, 2); cache.get(1); cache.put(3, 3); cache.get(2); cache.put(4, 4); cache.get(1); cache.get(3); cache.get(4)
examples.explanation: Initialize cache capacity=2. put(1,1): cache=[(1,1)]. put(2,2): cache=[(2,2),(1,1)]. get(1): scan cache, find (1,1) at index 1, return 1, move to front: cache=[(1,1),(2,2)]. put(3,3): at capacity=2, remove last (2,2), insert (3,3) at front: cache=[(3,3),(1,1)]. get(2): scan cache, not found, return -1. put(4,4): at capacity, remove last (1,1), insert (4,4) at front: cache=[(4,4),(3,3)]. get(1): scan, not found, return -1. get(3): scan, find (3,3) at index 1, return 3, move to front: cache=[(3,3),(4,4)]. get(4): scan, find (4,4) at index 1, return 4, move to front: cache=[(4,4),(3,3)].

examples.input: cache = LRUCache(3); cache.put(1, 10); cache.put(2, 20); cache.put(3, 30); cache.get(2); cache.put(4, 40); cache.get(1); cache.get(3); cache.get(4)
examples.explanation: Initialize capacity=3. put(1,10): cache=[(1,10)]. put(2,20): cache=[(2,20),(1,10)]. put(3,30): cache=[(3,30),(2,20),(1,10)]. get(2): scan, find (2,20) at index 1, return 20, move to front: cache=[(2,20),(3,30),(1,10)]. put(4,40): at capacity, remove last (1,10), insert (4,40) at front: cache=[(4,40),(2,20),(3,30)]. get(1): scan, not found, return -1. get(3): scan, find (3,30) at index 2, return 30, move to front: cache=[(3,30),(4,40),(2,20)]. get(4): scan, find (4,40) at index 1, return 40, move to front: cache=[(4,40),(3,30),(2,20)].

approaches.name: Approach 1: Naive Array O(n) Operations
approaches.summary: Use an array to store cache entries, scanning linearly for each get and put operation to update LRU order. This is straightforward but inefficient for large caches.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Implement LRU Cache with Array O(n) Operations
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of LRU Cache using array with linear scans for all operations.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete LRU Cache implementation using a naive array approach. The intuition is straightforward: we maintain an array of (key, value) pairs where the most recently used items are at the front. For get operations, we scan the array to find the key, return its value if found, and move it to the front to mark it as recently used. For put operations, we first check if the key exists by scanning; if it exists, we update its value and move it to the front. If it doesn't exist and we're at capacity, we remove the last element (least recently used) before inserting the new item at the front. This approach is intuitive but inefficient because every operation requires scanning the entire array in worst case. The algorithm: 1) Initialize cache with capacity, 2) For get(key): scan array for key, if found return value and move to front, else return -1, 3) For put(key, value): scan for key, if found update value and move to front, else if at capacity remove last element, then insert new (key, value) at front. Time complexity is O(n) for both get and put due to linear scans. Space complexity is O(n) for storing n items. This connects to the parent problem by showing how LRU can be implemented simply, though inefficiently. Common mistakes include forgetting to update LRU order on get operations, incorrect capacity handling, and off-by-one errors in array manipulation. Real code pattern: class LRUCache: def __init__(self, capacity): self.capacity = capacity; self.cache = [] def get(self, key): for i in range(len(self.cache)): if self.cache[i][0] == key: val = self.cache[i][1]; self.cache = [self.cache[i]] + self.cache[:i] + self.cache[i+1:]; return val; return -1 def put(self, key, value): for i in range(len(self.cache)): if self.cache[i][0] == key: self.cache[i] = (key, value); self.cache = [self.cache[i]] + self.cache[:i] + self.cache[i+1:]; return; if len(self.cache) == self.capacity: self.cache.pop(); self.cache.insert(0, (key, value))

examples.input: cache = LRUCache(2); cache.put(1, 1); cache.put(2, 2); cache.get(1); cache.put(3, 3); cache.get(2); cache.put(4, 4); cache.get(1); cache.get(3); cache.get(4)
examples.trace: Initialize cache capacity=2. put(1,1): cache=[(1,1)]. put(2,2): cache=[(2,2),(1,1)]. get(1): scan cache, find (1,1) at index 1, return 1, move to front: cache=[(1,1),(2,2)]. put(3,3): at capacity=2, remove last (2,2), insert (3,3) at front: cache=[(3,3),(1,1)]. get(2): scan cache, not found, return -1. put(4,4): at capacity, remove last (1,1), insert (4,4) at front: cache=[(4,4),(3,3)]. get(1): scan, not found, return -1. get(3): scan, find (3,3) at index 1, return 3, move to front: cache=[(3,3),(4,4)]. get(4): scan, find (4,4) at index 1, return 4, move to front: cache=[(4,4),(3,3)].

examples.input: cache = LRUCache(3); cache.put(1, 10); cache.put(2, 20); cache.put(3, 30); cache.get(2); cache.put(4, 40); cache.get(1); cache.get(3); cache.get(4)
examples.trace: Initialize capacity=3. put(1,10): cache=[(1,10)]. put(2,20): cache=[(2,20),(1,10)]. put(3,30): cache=[(3,30),(2,20),(1,10)]. get(2): scan, find (2,20) at index 1, return 20, move to front: cache=[(2,20),(3,30),(1,10)]. put(4,40): at capacity, remove last (1,10), insert (4,40) at front: cache=[(4,40),(2,20),(3,30)]. get(1): scan, not found, return -1. get(3): scan, find (3,30) at index 2, return 30, move to front: cache=[(3,30),(4,40),(2,20)]. get(4): scan, find (4,40) at index 1, return 40, move to front: cache=[(4,40),(3,30),(2,20)].

testCases.input: cache = LRUCache(1); cache.put(1, 1); cache.get(1); cache.put(2, 2); cache.get(1); cache.get(2)
cache = LRUCache(2); cache.put(1, 1); cache.put(2, 2); cache.get(1); cache.put(3, 3); cache.get(2); cache.put(4, 4); cache.get(1); cache.get(3); cache.get(4)
cache = LRUCache(3); cache.put(1, 10); cache.put(2, 20); cache.put(3, 30); cache.get(2); cache.put(4, 40); cache.get(1); cache.get(3); cache.get(4)
cache = LRUCache(2); cache.get(1); cache.put(1, 1); cache.put(2, 2); cache.get(1); cache.put(3, 3); cache.get(2)
cache = LRUCache(3); cache.put(1, 1); cache.put(2, 2); cache.put(3, 3); cache.put(4, 4); cache.get(1); cache.get(2); cache.get(3); cache.get(4)
cache = LRUCache(2); cache.put(1, 1); cache.put(2, 2); cache.get(1); cache.put(1, 10); cache.get(1); cache.get(2)
cache = LRUCache(5); for i in range(5): cache.put(i, i*10); cache.get(0); cache.put(5, 50); cache.get(1); cache.get(0); cache.get(5)
cache = LRUCache(2); cache.put(1, 1); cache.put(2, 2); cache.get(1); cache.put(3, 3); cache.get(2); cache.put(4, 4); cache.get(1); cache.get(3); cache.get(4)

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Linear Scan and Move to Front in Array
ladders.desc: Implement the core operation of scanning array for a key and moving it to front.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the naive LRU Cache approach. The intuition is that to maintain LRU order, whenever we access an item (via get or put), we need to mark it as most recently used by moving it to the front of the array. This requires first finding the item by linear scan, then reorganizing the array. The algorithm: 1) Scan the array from start to end, comparing each key with the target key. 2) If found at index i, store its value, then create a new array with that element at position 0, followed by elements before i, then elements after i. 3) If not found, return a sentinel value (like -1). Time complexity is O(n) for scanning and O(n) for array reconstruction (though this can be optimized with list slicing). Space complexity is O(n) for the new array. This connects to L0 by being the fundamental operation called in both get and put methods. Common mistakes include incorrect index handling when reconstructing the array, forgetting to return the value when found, and inefficient array operations. Real code pattern: def find_and_move_to_front(arr, key): for i in range(len(arr)): if arr[i][0] == key: val = arr[i][1]; new_arr = [arr[i]] + arr[:i] + arr[i+1:]; return (val, new_arr); return (-1, arr).

examples.trace: Scan arr: index 0: (2,20) key 2 != 1. index 1: (1,10) key 1 == 1, found. Store val=10. Create new_arr: [arr[1]] = [(1,10)]; arr[:1] = [(2,20)]; arr[2:] = [(3,30)]; concatenate: [(1,10), (2,20), (3,30)]. Return (10, new_arr).
Scan arr: index 0: (5,50) key 5 != 6. index 1: (4,40) key 4 != 6. index 2: (3,30) key 3 != 6. Not found. Return (-1, original arr unchanged).

testCases.input: arr = [(a, a*10) for a in range(5)]; key = 2

ladders.title: Core Logic — Maintaining LRU Order with Array Front as MRU
ladders.desc: Understand how array position represents recency, with front as most recently used.
ladders.explanation: The core insight of this approach is using array ordering to track recency: the most recently used (MRU) item is always at index 0, and the least recently used (LRU) item is at the end. This is intuitive because arrays have inherent order, and moving items to the front is a simple way to mark them as recent. The logic: 1) On any access (get or put update), move the item to index 0. 2) When inserting new items, add them at index 0. 3) When evicting due to capacity, remove from the end. This ensures the array always reflects LRU order without explicit timestamps. The algorithm maintains this invariant: after each operation, array[0] is MRU and array[-1] is LRU. Time complexity for maintaining order is O(n) due to linear scans and shifts. Space is O(n). This connects to L1 by providing the rationale for the move-to-front operation. Common mistakes include not maintaining the invariant consistently (e.g., forgetting to move on get), or misunderstanding that array order alone suffices without extra data structures. Real code pattern: The invariant is enforced by always using insert(0, item) for new puts, and reconstructing array on accesses.

examples.input: Start: cache=[(1,10), (2,20)] (1 is MRU, 2 is LRU). Operation: get(2).
examples.trace: Initial: MRU at index 0 is (1,10), LRU at index 1 is (2,20). get(2): find key 2 at index 1, move to front. New cache: [(2,20), (1,10)]. Now MRU is (2,20), LRU is (1,10).

examples.input: Start: cache=[(3,30), (2,20), (1,10)] at capacity 3. Operation: put(4,40).
examples.trace: Initial: MRU (3,30), LRU (1,10). put(4,40): at capacity, evict LRU (1,10) from end, insert new (4,40) at front. New cache: [(4,40), (3,30), (2,20)]. MRU is (4,40), LRU is (2,20).

testCases.input: cache=[(a, a) for a in range(3)]; get(0); get(2)

ladders.title: Building Block — Array Search and Update with Linear Scan
ladders.desc: Implement linear search in array to find a key and optionally update its value.
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on searching an array of (key, value) pairs for a specific key using linear scan, and updating its value if found. The intuition is simple: we iterate through each element, compare keys, and take action if a match is found. This is fundamental because both get and put operations in LRU Cache require finding whether a key exists. The algorithm: 1) Iterate i from 0 to len(arr)-1. 2) At each index, check if arr[i][0] equals target key. 3) If found, we can return its index and value, or update the value in place. 4) If not found after loop, return a sentinel (e.g., -1). Time complexity is O(n) for the scan. Space complexity is O(1) extra space. This connects to L2 by being the first step in maintaining LRU order—we need to find an item before moving it. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loop bounds, not handling empty arrays, and confusing key with value. Real code pattern: def linear_search(arr, key): for i in range(len(arr)): if arr[i][0] == key: return i, arr[i][1]; return -1, None. For update: if i != -1: arr[i] = (key, new_value).

examples.trace: Scan arr: i=0: (1,10) key 1 != 2. i=1: (2,20) key 2 == 2, found. Return index 1, value 20.

examples.input: arr = [(5,50), (4,40)]; key = 3; update to value 30 if found
examples.trace: Scan arr: i=0: (5,50) key 5 != 3. i=1: (4,40) key 4 != 3. Not found. Return -1, None. No update.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Insert and Remove Elements from Array Ends
ladders.desc: Perform basic array operations: insert at front and remove from end.
ladders.explanation: This level covers fundamental array manipulations: inserting an element at the beginning and removing from the end. These are basic operations because in the LRU Cache array approach, new items are added at the front (to mark as MRU) and evictions happen from the end (LRU). The intuition is that arrays support these operations, though with potential O(n) time for inserts due to shifting. For insert at front: create a new list with the new element followed by all existing elements. For remove from end: use pop() to remove and return the last element. The algorithm: 1) Insert: new_arr = [new_item] + arr. 2) Remove: if arr not empty, removed = arr.pop(); return removed. Time complexity: insert is O(n) due to creating new list; remove is O(1) for pop from end. Space complexity: insert uses O(n) extra space; remove uses O(1). This connects to L3 by being the mechanical steps after searching—once we find or don't find a key, we insert or remove accordingly. Common mistakes include modifying arrays in place incorrectly, or using inefficient methods like repeated inserts. Real code pattern: def insert_front(arr, item): return [item] + arr. def remove_end(arr): if arr: return arr.pop(); else return None.

examples.input: arr = [(1,10), (2,20)]; insert (3,30) at front
arr = [(5,50), (4,40), (3,30)]; remove from end
examples.trace: Start arr = [(5,50), (4,40), (3,30)]. pop() removes last element (3,30) and returns it. New arr = [(5,50), (4,40)].

testCases.input: arr = [(a, a*10) for a in range(3)]; insert (10,100) at front
arr = [(5,5), (4,4), (3,3)]; remove from end; then insert (6,6) at front
arr = [(1,1), (2,2)]; insert (3,3) at front; remove from end
arr = [(10,1000)]; remove from end; insert (20,2000) at front

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array of Key-Value Pairs and Capacity Limit
ladders.desc: Understand storing data as (key, value) pairs in an array with a size limit.
ladders.explanation: This is the foundational concept: using an array to store key-value pairs, with a fixed capacity limiting the number of pairs. The intuition is that arrays are simple data structures that hold ordered elements; here, each element is a tuple (key, value). The capacity defines the maximum number of pairs allowed—if exceeded, we must evict old pairs. This is basic because it's the starting point for any cache implementation. The algorithm involves: 1) Initializing an empty array and a capacity integer. 2) Adding pairs to the array up to capacity. 3) Enforcing the limit by checking array length before additions. Time complexity for basic operations is O(1) for length check, but overall cache operations will be higher. Space complexity is O(n) for n pairs. This connects to L4 by providing the data structure on which insert/remove operations are performed. Common mistakes include not initializing capacity correctly, or misunderstanding that array stores pairs, not just values. Real code pattern: class SimpleCache: def __init__(self, capacity): self.capacity = capacity; self.items = [] def add(self, key, value): if len(self.items) < self.capacity: self.items.append((key, value)).

examples.input: capacity = 2; add (1,10); add (2,20); try add (3,30)
examples.trace: Initialize cache with capacity=2, items=[]. add(1,10): items length 0 < 2, append (1,10): items=[(1,10)]. add(2,20): length 1 < 2, append (2,20): items=[(1,10),(2,20)]. add(3,30): length 2 == capacity, do not add. Final items=[(1,10),(2,20)].

examples.input: capacity = 3; add (a, a*10) for a in [1,2,3,4]

testCases.input: capacity = 3; add (a, a) for a in [1,2,3]
capacity = 5; add (i, i*100) for i in range(6)
capacity = 2; add (1,1); add (2,2); add (1,10) (update not allowed here)
capacity = 3; add (x, x*x) for x in [1,2]
capacity = 10; add (n, n*10) for n in range(15)

approaches.name: Approach 2: HashMap with Doubly Linked List O(1)
approaches.summary: Combine a hashmap for O(1) access with a doubly linked list to maintain LRU order, moving nodes to the front on access and evicting from the tail when capacity is exceeded. This is the standard optimal solution.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Complete LRU Cache Implementation
ladders.desc: Implement LRU Cache with O(1) get and put operations using hashmap and doubly linked list
ladders.explanation: The LRU (Least Recently Used) cache problem requires maintaining a fixed-size cache where the least recently accessed item is evicted when capacity is exceeded. The optimal solution combines two data structures: a hashmap for O(1) key lookup and a doubly linked list (DLL) for maintaining access order. The hashmap maps keys to their corresponding nodes in the DLL. When a key is accessed via get() or updated via put(), its node is moved to the front (most recent position) of the DLL. When capacity is exceeded during put(), the tail node (least recently used) is removed from both the DLL and hashmap. The intuition is that the hashmap provides instant access to any node, while the DLL maintains temporal ordering without requiring expensive shifting operations. The doubly linked list allows O(1) removal of any node (given a reference) and O(1) insertion at the head. Time complexity is O(1) for both operations because hashmap operations are O(1) on average and DLL operations are O(1) with proper node references. Space complexity is O(n) where n is capacity, storing n nodes in DLL and n entries in hashmap. Connection to L1: This full implementation relies on the key subroutine of moving nodes to the front. Common mistakes include not updating both hashmap and DLL consistently, forgetting to check capacity before insertion, and improper handling of edge cases like updating existing keys. Real code pattern involves maintaining head/tail pointers, a size counter, and implementing helper methods for adding/removing nodes.

examples.input:
cache = LRUCache(2)
put(1,1)
put(2,2)
get(1)
put(3,3)
get(2)
put(4,4)
get(1)
get(3)
get(4)
examples.trace:
1. Initialize cache with capacity=2, size=0, empty hashmap, empty DLL
2. put(1,1): Create node(1,1), add to front: DLL: 1, hashmap: {1:node1}, size=1
3. put(2,2): Create node(2,2), add to front: DLL: 2↔1, hashmap: {1:node1,2:node2}, size=2
4. get(1): Find node1 via hashmap, move to front: DLL: 1↔2, return 1
5. put(3,3): Capacity full (size=2), evict tail node2 (LRU), remove from hashmap, create node3, add to front: DLL: 3↔1, hashmap: {1:node1,3:node3}, size=2
6. get(2): Key 2 not in hashmap, return -1
7. put(4,4): Capacity full, evict tail node1, remove from hashmap, create node4, add to front: DLL: 4↔3, hashmap: {3:node3,4:node4}, size=2
8. get(1): Key 1 not in hashmap, return -1
9. get(3): Find node3, move to front: DLL: 3↔4, return 3
10. get(4): Find node4, move to front: DLL: 4↔3, return 4

examples.input:
cache = LRUCache(1)
put(2,1)
get(2)
put(3,2)
get(2)
get(3)
examples.trace:
1. Initialize capacity=1, size=0
2. put(2,1): Add node2 to front: DLL: 2, hashmap: {2:node2}, size=1
3. get(2): Find node2, already at front, return 1
4. put(3,2): Capacity full, evict node2, remove from hashmap, add node3: DLL: 3, hashmap: {3:node3}, size=1
5. get(2): Key 2 not in hashmap, return -1
6. get(3): Find node3, return 2

testCases.input: cache = LRUCache(2); operations: [put(1,1), put(2,2), get(1), put(3,3), get(2), put(4,4), get(1), get(3), get(4)]
cache = LRUCache(1); operations: [put(2,1), get(2), put(3,2), get(2), get(3)]
cache = LRUCache(3); operations: [put(1,10), put(2,20), put(3,30), get(1), put(4,40), get(2), get(3), get(4)]
cache = LRUCache(2); operations: [put(1,1), put(2,2), put(1,10), get(1), get(2)]
cache = LRUCache(2); operations: [get(1), put(1,1), put(2,2), get(1), put(3,3), get(2)]
cache = LRUCache(4); operations: [put(1,1), put(2,2), put(3,3), put(4,4), get(1), get(2), put(5,5), get(3)]
cache = LRUCache(2); operations: [put(1,1), put(2,2), get(1), put(1,10), get(1), get(2)]
cache = LRUCache(3); operations: [put(1,1), put(2,2), put(3,3), get(1), get(2), get(3), put(4,4), get(1)]
cache = LRUCache(2); operations: [put(1,1), put(2,2), put(3,3), put(4,4), get(1), get(2), get(3), get(4)]

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Move Node to Front Operation
ladders.desc: Implement the operation that moves a node to the front of the doubly linked list
ladders.explanation: The move-to-front operation is the core mechanism that maintains LRU ordering. Whenever a key is accessed (via get or put), its corresponding node must become the most recently used by moving it to the head of the doubly linked list. The intuition is that by keeping the most recently accessed items at the front and least recently accessed at the back, we can easily identify which item to evict when capacity is exceeded. The algorithm works in three cases: 1) Node is already at head (do nothing), 2) Node is at tail (special handling needed), 3) Node is in middle. For case 2 and 3, we remove the node from its current position and insert it at the head. The step-by-step process: First, check if node is already head - if yes, return. Otherwise, disconnect the node from its current position by updating its neighbors' pointers. If node is tail, update tail to node's prev. Then insert node at head: set node's next to current head, node's prev to null, update old head's prev to node, and update head pointer to node. Time complexity is O(1) as it involves only pointer manipulations. Space complexity is O(1) as no additional data structures are created. Connection to L0: This subroutine is called every time get() or put() accesses an existing key. Common mistakes include not handling all three cases separately, forgetting to update tail pointer when moving the tail node, and not properly updating both prev and next pointers of neighboring nodes. Real code pattern involves checking node.prev to determine if node is head, and node.next to determine if special tail handling is needed.

examples.input: DLL: 3↔2↔1 (head=3, tail=1), move node with value 2 to front
examples.trace:
1. Initial: head=node3, tail=node1, node2.prev=node3, node2.next=node1
2. Node2 is not head (node2.prev exists)
3. Node2 is not tail (node2.next exists)
4. Remove node2: node3.next = node1, node1.prev = node3
5. Insert at head: node2.next = node3, node2.prev = null, node3.prev = node2
6. Update head = node2
7. Final: head=node2, node2.next=node3, node3.next=node1, node1.prev=node3, tail=node1

examples.input: DLL: 2↔1 (head=2, tail=1), move node with value 1 (tail) to front
examples.trace:
1. Initial: head=node2, tail=node1, node1.prev=node2, node1.next=null
2. Node1 is not head (node1.prev exists)
3. Node1 is tail (node1.next is null)
4. Remove node1: node2.next = null
5. Update tail = node2
6. Insert at head: node1.next = node2, node1.prev = null, node2.prev = node1
7. Update head = node1
8. Final: head=node1, node1.next=node2, node2.prev=node1, tail=node2

ladders.title: Core Logic — Maintaining Order with DLL and Eviction Strategy
ladders.desc: Understand how doubly linked list maintains access order and determines which item to evict
ladders.explanation: The core insight of the LRU cache solution is using a doubly linked list to maintain temporal ordering of cache items, with the hashmap providing O(1) access to any node. The DLL represents the usage timeline: head is most recently used, tail is least recently used. Every access moves the corresponding node to the head. When capacity is exceeded, we evict the tail node. The intuition is that the tail naturally becomes the least recently used item because: 1) New items are added at head, 2) Accessed items move to head, 3) Items that aren't accessed gradually move toward tail as other items are accessed. The eviction strategy is simple: when size > capacity, remove tail node from DLL and its key from hashmap. The algorithm maintains two invariants: 1) DLL always contains nodes in LRU order (head=MRU, tail=LRU), 2) Hashmap keys correspond exactly to DLL nodes. Time complexity for maintaining order is O(1) per operation because DLL insertion/deletion at head/tail is O(1) with proper pointers. Space complexity is O(n) for storing the DLL. Connection to L1: The move-to-front operation is what maintains the ordering invariant. Common mistakes include not updating both data structures consistently, evicting wrong node (not tail), and not maintaining size counter properly. Real code pattern involves always adding new nodes at head, moving accessed nodes to head, and evicting from tail when capacity exceeded.

examples.input: Capacity=2, operations: put(1,A), put(2,B), get(1), put(3,C)
examples.trace:
1. put(1,A): Add at head: DLL: A, hashmap: {1:A}, size=1
2. put(2,B): Add at head: DLL: B↔A, hashmap: {1:A,2:B}, size=2
3. get(1): Move A to head: DLL: A↔B, hashmap unchanged
4. put(3,C): Capacity full (size=2), evict tail B (LRU), remove key 2, add C at head: DLL: C↔A, hashmap: {1:A,3:C}, size=2
Key insight: After get(1), A became MRU, B became LRU at tail, so B was evicted

examples.input: Capacity=3, operations: put(1,X), put(2,Y), put(3,Z), get(2), put(4,W)
examples.trace:
1. put(1,X): DLL: X, size=1
2. put(2,Y): DLL: Y↔X, size=2
3. put(3,Z): DLL: Z↔Y↔X, size=3
4. get(2): Move Y to head: DLL: Y↔Z↔X
5. put(4,W): Capacity full, evict tail X (LRU), add W at head: DLL: W↔Y↔Z, hashmap: {2:Y,3:Z,4:W}
Key insight: X remained at tail throughout because it was never accessed after initial insertion

testCases.input: Capacity=3, operations: put(1,x), put(2,y), get(1), put(3,z), put(4,w)
Capacity=2, operations: put(1,a), get(1), put(2,b), put(3,c)
Capacity=3, operations: put(1,a), put(2,b), put(3,c), get(1), get(2), put(4,d)
Capacity=2, operations: put(1,a), put(2,b), put(1,c), put(3,d)
Capacity=4, operations: put(1,a), put(2,b), put(3,c), put(4,d), get(1), put(5,e)
Capacity=2, operations: put(1,a), put(2,b), get(2), put(3,c)
Capacity=3, operations: put(1,a), put(2,b), put(3,c), get(3), get(2), get(1), put(4,d)
Capacity=2, operations: put(1,a), get(1), put(2,b), get(2), put(3,c)

ladders.title: Building Block — Doubly Linked List Operations
ladders.desc: Implement basic doubly linked list operations: insert at head, remove node, get tail
ladders.explanation: Doubly linked list (DLL) is chosen over singly linked list because it allows O(1) removal of any node given a reference to that node. In LRU cache, we need to frequently move nodes from middle/tail to head, which requires removing them from current position. A DLL has each node containing prev and next pointers, enabling bidirectional traversal. The three fundamental operations needed are: 1) Insert node at head - makes new node the most recent, 2) Remove node - extracts a node from any position, 3) Get tail - identifies the least recent node for eviction. For insert at head: Create new node, set its next to current head, set its prev to null. If list not empty, set old head's prev to new node. Update head pointer to new node. If list was empty, also set tail to new node. For remove node: Handle three cases - node is head, node is tail, node is in middle. Update neighbors' pointers to bypass the node. If node was head, update head to node.next. If node was tail, update tail to node.prev. For get tail: Simply return tail pointer. Time complexity: All operations are O(1) as they involve constant pointer updates. Space complexity: O(1) for operations, O(n) for storing the list. Connection to L2: These operations enable maintaining LRU order - insert at head for new/accessed items, remove for eviction, get tail to identify LRU item. Common mistakes include not handling empty list case in insertion, not updating both prev and next pointers in removal, and forgetting to update head/tail pointers when they're affected. Real code pattern involves maintaining head and tail pointers as class members, and carefully checking for null pointers.

examples.input: DLL: 2↔1 (head=2, tail=1), insert node with value 3 at head
examples.trace:
1. Create node3 with value=3, prev=null, next=null
2. Current head is node2
3. Set node3.next = node2
4. Set node2.prev = node3
5. Update head = node3
6. Tail remains node1
7. Final: node3.next=node2, node2.prev=node3, node2.next=node1, node1.prev=node2

examples.input: DLL: 3↔2↔1 (head=3, tail=1), remove node with value 2
examples.trace:
1. Identify node2 (between node3 and node1)
2. Node2 is not head (prev exists), not tail (next exists)
3. Remove node2: node3.next = node1, node1.prev = node3
4. Set node2.prev = null, node2.next = null (optional)
5. Head remains node3, tail remains node1
6. Final: node3.next=node1, node1.prev=node3

testCases.input: Empty DLL, insert node 5 at head

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Hashmap Lookup and Update
ladders.desc: Use hashmap to store and retrieve key-node mappings in O(1) time
ladders.explanation: A hashmap (dictionary) provides O(1) average time complexity for insert, lookup, and delete operations. In LRU cache, the hashmap stores mappings from keys to their corresponding nodes in the doubly linked list. This allows us to instantly locate any node given its key. The basic operations needed are: 1) Get - retrieve value for a key, 2) Put - insert or update key-value pair, 3) Remove - delete a key-value pair. For get(key): Check if key exists in hashmap. If not, return sentinel value (-1). If exists, return the node's value and trigger move-to-front operation (in higher level). For put(key, value): If key exists, update node's value and move to front. If key doesn't exist, create new node, add to hashmap, insert at head of DLL, and check capacity. For remove(key): Delete key from hashmap and remove corresponding node from DLL. The intuition is that hashmap provides direct access to any node without traversing the DLL. Time complexity is O(1) on average for hashmap operations. Space complexity is O(n) for storing n key-value pairs. Connection to L3: The hashmap works with DLL - hashmap stores references to DLL nodes, enabling O(1) access to any node. Common mistakes include not checking for key existence before operations, not handling hashmap and DLL synchronization, and assuming hashmap operations are always O(1) (they're amortized O(1)). Real code pattern uses dictionary with key as integer and value as node reference.

examples.input: Hashmap: {1:nodeA, 2:nodeB}, get key 1
examples.trace:
1. Check if key 1 exists in hashmap: yes
2. Retrieve nodeA reference
3. Access nodeA.value (e.g., 10)
4. Return 10
Note: In full LRU, would also move nodeA to front

examples.input: Hashmap: {1:nodeX}, put key 2 with value 20
examples.trace:
1. Check if key 2 exists: no
2. Create new nodeY with value=20
3. Add to hashmap: hashmap[2] = nodeY
4. In full LRU: would also insert nodeY at head of DLL
5. Hashmap now contains both mappings

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Cache and LRU Policy
ladders.desc: Learn what a cache is and how Least Recently Used (LRU) eviction works
ladders.explanation: A cache is a temporary storage that holds frequently accessed data to speed up future requests. It sits between a fast component (like CPU) and a slow component (like disk or database). When data is requested, the cache is checked first. If found (cache hit), it's returned quickly. If not found (cache miss), it's fetched from slow storage and stored in cache for future requests. Caches have limited capacity, so when full, they must decide which item to remove (evict) to make space for new items. This decision is made by an eviction policy. LRU (Least Recently Used) is one such policy that removes the item that hasn't been accessed for the longest time. The intuition is that recently used items are likely to be used again soon (temporal locality principle). Imagine a bookshelf with limited space: when you need space for a new book, you remove the book you haven't read in the longest time. LRU implementation needs to track access order: every time an item is accessed (read or written), it becomes 'most recently used.' The item that hasn't been accessed for the longest time becomes 'least recently used' and is evicted when space is needed. Time complexity considerations: Naive implementations might use arrays or lists with O(n) operations, but optimal implementation needs O(1) operations. Space complexity is O(capacity) for storing cache items. Connection to L4: The hashmap and DLL data structures are chosen to implement LRU policy efficiently. Common mistakes include confusing LRU with other policies like FIFO or LFU, not understanding why LRU needs to track both reads and writes, and not recognizing that cache operations should be fast. Real-world analogy: Think of a clothing closet - you wear (access) clothes, and when closet is full, you donate the clothes you haven't worn in longest time.

examples.input: Cache capacity=3, items: A,B,C (A oldest, C newest). Access pattern: Read A, Add D
examples.trace:
1. Initial cache: [A,B,C] with A oldest (LRU), C newest (MRU)
2. Read A: A becomes most recent
3. New order: [B,C,A] with B oldest, A newest
4. Add D: Cache full (capacity=3)
5. Evict oldest item: B
6. New cache: [C,A,D] with C oldest, D newest
Key insight: Reading A made it recent, so B became LRU

examples.input: Cache capacity=2, items: X,Y. Access pattern: Add Z
examples.trace:
1. Initial: [X,Y] with X oldest, Y newest
2. Add Z: Cache full
3. Evict oldest: X
4. New cache: [Y,Z] with Y oldest, Z newest
Key insight: When adding new item to full cache, always evict LRU item

testCases.input: Capacity=2, initial: [A,B], access: Read A, Add C
Capacity=3, initial: [X,Y,Z], access: Read Y, Add W
Capacity=2, initial: [M,N], access: Read M, Read N, Add O
Capacity=3, initial: [1,2,3], access: Read 1, Read 2, Add 4
Capacity=3, initial: [X,Y,Z], access: Read X, Read Z, Add W
Capacity=2, initial: empty, access: Add P, Add Q, Add R
Capacity=3, initial: [A,B,C], access: Read B, Read A, Add D
Capacity=2, initial: [M,N], access: Read N, Add O

approaches.name: Approach 3: Ordered Dictionary O(1)
approaches.summary: Leverage a built-in ordered dictionary (e.g., Python's OrderedDict or Java's LinkedHashMap) that maintains insertion order, simplifying implementation by handling LRU updates internally. This is practical in languages with such data structures.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Implement LRU Cache with Ordered Dictionary
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of LRU Cache using an ordered dictionary that maintains insertion order.
ladders.explanation: The LRU (Least Recently Used) cache problem requires implementing a data structure with O(1) get and put operations while maintaining capacity constraints. The ordered dictionary approach leverages built-in data structures like Python's OrderedDict or Java's LinkedHashMap that maintain insertion order, making LRU updates straightforward. The intuition is that the ordered dictionary automatically tracks access order - when we get or update a key, we move it to the end (most recent), and when we need to evict, we remove from the beginning (least recent). This eliminates manual DLL manipulation. The algorithm: 1) Initialize cache with capacity and ordered dictionary. 2) For get(key): if key exists, move to end (mark as recent) and return value; else return -1. 3) For put(key, value): if key exists, update value and move to end; else if at capacity, pop first item (LRU), then add new key-value at end. Time complexity is O(1) for both operations since ordered dictionary operations are O(1). Space is O(n) where n ≤ capacity. Connection to L1: This full solution uses the key subroutine of moving items to mark as recent. Common mistakes include forgetting to update order on get operations, not handling capacity correctly, or using wrong ordered dictionary methods. Real pattern: class LRUCache: def __init__(self, capacity): self.capacity = capacity; self.cache = OrderedDict(). def get(self, key): if key not in self.cache: return -1; self.cache.move_to_end(key); return self.cache[key]. def put(self, key, value): if key in self.cache: self.cache.move_to_end(key); self.cache[key] = value; else: if len(self.cache) >= self.capacity: self.cache.popitem(last=False); self.cache[key] = value.

examples.input: cache = LRUCache(2); cache.put(1, 1); cache.put(2, 2); cache.get(1); cache.put(3, 3); cache.get(2); cache.put(4, 4); cache.get(1); cache.get(3); cache.get(4);
examples.trace: Initialize cache capacity=2. put(1,1): cache={1:1}. put(2,2): cache={1:1, 2:2}. get(1): key exists, move 1 to end, cache={2:2, 1:1}, return 1. put(3,3): at capacity, pop LRU (2), cache={1:1}, add 3, cache={1:1, 3:3}. get(2): key not found, return -1. put(4,4): at capacity, pop LRU (1), cache={3:3}, add 4, cache={3:3, 4:4}. get(1): key not found, return -1. get(3): key exists, move to end, cache={4:4, 3:3}, return 3. get(4): key exists, move to end, cache={3:3, 4:4}, return 4.

examples.input: cache = LRUCache(1); cache.put(2, 1); cache.get(2); cache.put(3, 2); cache.get(2); cache.get(3);
examples.trace: Initialize capacity=1. put(2,1): cache={2:1}. get(2): key exists, move to end (only item), cache={2:1}, return 1. put(3,2): at capacity, pop LRU (2), cache={}, add 3, cache={3:2}. get(2): key not found, return -1. get(3): key exists, move to end, cache={3:2}, return 2.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Move-to-End Operation in Ordered Dictionary
ladders.desc: Implement the operation that moves a key to the end of an ordered dictionary to mark it as recently used.
ladders.explanation: The move-to-end operation is crucial for maintaining LRU order in the ordered dictionary approach. The intuition is that whenever we access a key (via get or update), we need to mark it as the most recently used item. In an ordered dictionary that maintains insertion order, moving an existing key to the end effectively makes it the newest item. This operation ensures that the dictionary order reflects usage recency - items at the beginning are least recent, items at the end are most recent. The algorithm: 1) Check if key exists in dictionary. 2) Remove the key-value pair from its current position. 3) Re-insert it at the end. Time complexity is O(1) since ordered dictionary removal and insertion at ends are O(1). Space is O(1) extra. Connection to L0: This subroutine is called in both get and put operations to maintain LRU order. Common mistakes include not checking if key exists before moving, or using incorrect methods (like pop then reinsert instead of dedicated move_to_end). Real pattern: def move_to_recent(cache, key): if key in cache: value = cache[key]; del cache[key]; cache[key] = value. Or using built-in: cache.move_to_end(key).

examples.input: cache = OrderedDict([(1,'a'), (2,'b'), (3,'c')]); move_to_recent(cache, 2); cache
examples.trace: Start: cache order = 1→2→3. Key 2 exists at position 2. Remove key 2: cache becomes 1→3. Reinsert 2 at end: cache becomes 1→3→2. Final order: 1,3,2.

examples.input: cache = OrderedDict([(5,'x')]); move_to_recent(cache, 5); cache
examples.trace: Start: cache order = 5. Key 5 exists. Remove key 5: cache becomes empty. Reinsert 5 at end: cache becomes 5. Final order unchanged since only one item.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Maintaining LRU Order with Ordered Dictionary
ladders.desc: Understand how ordered dictionary insertion order naturally tracks least/most recently used items.
ladders.explanation: The core insight is that an ordered dictionary maintaining insertion order can efficiently track LRU information without additional data structures. The intuition: when we insert or update items, they go to the end (most recent). When we need to evict, we remove from the beginning (least recent). The dictionary order becomes a timeline of usage - older items drift left, newer items stay right. This works because: 1) Initial inserts create order. 2) Get operations move items to end (refreshing recency). 3) Put updates move to end. 4) Capacity eviction removes from front. The algorithm mentally: maintain dictionary D with capacity C. On operation: if get(k): if k in D, move k to end, return value. if put(k,v): if k in D, update v, move to end; else if len(D)=C, pop first item, add (k,v) to end. Time O(1), space O(C). Connection to L1: This logic uses move-to-end as building block. Common mistakes: misunderstanding that ordered dictionary tracks access order automatically (it doesn't - we must explicitly move on gets), or confusing order with sorted keys. Real pattern: The ordered dictionary is not self-maintaining - we must call move_to_end on accesses.

examples.input: Capacity=2. Operations: put(1,A), put(2,B), get(1), put(3,C). Show dictionary order after each step.
examples.trace: Step1: put(1,A) → add to end: order=[1]. Step2: put(2,B) → add to end: order=[1,2]. Step3: get(1) → exists, move 1 to end: order=[2,1]. Step4: put(3,C) → at capacity, pop front (2), add 3 to end: order=[1,3].

examples.input: Capacity=3. Operations: put(1,X), put(2,Y), put(3,Z), get(2), put(4,W). Show order.
examples.trace: Step1: put(1,X) → order=[1]. Step2: put(2,Y) → order=[1,2]. Step3: put(3,Z) → order=[1,2,3]. Step4: get(2) → move to end: order=[1,3,2]. Step5: put(4,W) → at capacity, pop front (1), add to end: order=[3,2,4].

testCases.input: Capacity=2; operations: put(1,10), put(2,20), get(1), put(3,30); final order of keys
Capacity=1; operations: put(1,10), get(1), put(2,20); final order
Capacity=3; operations: put(1,10), put(2,20), put(3,30), get(2), put(4,40); final order
Capacity=2; operations: put(1,10), put(2,20), get(2), put(3,30); final order
Capacity=2; operations: put(1,10), get(1), put(2,20), put(3,30); final order
Capacity=3; operations: put(1,10), put(2,20), get(1), get(2), put(3,30), put(4,40); final order
Capacity=2; operations: put(1,10), put(1,20), put(2,30); final order
Capacity=3; operations: put(1,10), put(2,20), put(3,30), get(1), get(2), get(3); final order
Capacity=2; operations: put(1,10), put(2,20), put(3,30), put(4,40); final order
Capacity=4; operations: put(1,10), put(2,20), put(3,30), get(1), put(4,40), put(5,50); final order

ladders.title: Building Block — Ordered Dictionary Operations: Pop First and Insert Last
ladders.desc: Master the two key ordered dictionary operations needed for LRU: remove least recent (pop first) and add most recent (insert last).
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on the two dictionary operations critical for LRU: popping the first item (least recent) and inserting at the end (most recent). The intuition is that the ordered dictionary acts as a queue where front=oldest, back=newest. Popping first removes the LRU item during eviction; inserting last adds new or updated items. These are O(1) operations in proper ordered dictionary implementations. Algorithm: 1) Pop first: cache.popitem(last=False) removes and returns the first key-value pair. 2) Insert last: cache[key] = value adds to end; if key exists, it updates value and position. Time O(1) each. Space O(1). Connection to L2: These operations implement the eviction and addition parts of LRU logic. Common mistakes: using pop() instead of popitem(last=False), or not handling empty dictionary on pop. Real pattern: def pop_first(cache): return cache.popitem(last=False). def insert_last(cache, key, value): cache[key] = value.

examples.input: cache = OrderedDict([(1,'a'), (2,'b'), (3,'c')]); pop_first(cache); cache
examples.trace: Start: order=1→2→3. pop_first removes first item (1,'a'). Remaining order=2→3. Returned (1,'a').

examples.input: cache = OrderedDict([(1,'x')]); insert_last(cache, 2, 'y'); cache
examples.trace: Start: order=1. insert_last(2,'y') adds to end. New order=1→2.

testCases.input: cache = OrderedDict([(1,10)]); insert_last(cache, 1, 100); list(cache.keys()) and cache[1]
cache = OrderedDict([(1,10),(2,20),(3,30)]); insert_last(cache, 2, 200); list(cache.keys()) and cache[2]

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Dictionary Get, Put, and Contains Check
ladders.desc: Fundamental dictionary operations: check if key exists, retrieve value, and insert/update key-value pairs.
ladders.explanation: This level covers basic dictionary operations that underpin the LRU cache. The intuition: a dictionary (hash map) provides O(1) access by key, which is essential for LRU's O(1) requirements. The operations: 1) Contains check: key in dict returns boolean. 2) Get value: dict[key] returns value or raises KeyError if missing. 3) Put/update: dict[key] = value inserts new key or updates existing. These are O(1) average case. Space O(n). Connection to L3: These basic ops are used within the ordered dictionary operations. Common mistakes: not checking existence before get (causing KeyError), or misunderstanding that dict[key]=value updates order in regular dict (it doesn't - ordered dict adds this). Real pattern: if key in d: val = d[key]. d[key] = value.

examples.input: d = {1:10, 2:20}; Check if 1 in d, get d[1], update d[1]=100, add d[3]=30
examples.trace: Step1: 1 in d → True. Step2: d[1] → 10. Step3: d[1]=100 updates value for key 1. Step4: d[3]=30 adds new key 3 with value 30.

examples.input: d = {}; Check if 5 in d, add d[5]=50, get d[5]
examples.trace: Step1: 5 in d → False. Step2: d[5]=50 adds key 5. Step3: d[5] returns 50.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Key-Value Pairs and Basic Data Storage
ladders.desc: Understand the fundamental concept of storing data as key-value pairs where each key maps to one value.
ladders.explanation: This is the foundational concept: key-value pairs are a way to organize data where each unique key is associated with a value. The intuition is like a dictionary where words (keys) have definitions (values). Keys must be unique within a collection; values can be duplicated. This structure allows efficient lookup by key. Simple examples: phone book (name→number), student records (ID→grade). In programming, this is implemented as hash maps/dictionaries. Basic operations: store pair (key,value), retrieve value by key, check if key exists. No order is guaranteed in basic dictionaries. Time: O(1) for lookup ideally. Space: O(n). Connection to L4: This concept underlies all dictionary operations. Common mistakes: thinking keys can be duplicate, or that values can be accessed without keys. Real pattern: Think of a real dictionary: word 'apple' maps to definition 'fruit'.

examples.input: Store pairs: (1,'apple'), (2,'banana'), (3,'cherry'). Retrieve value for key 2. Check if key 4 exists.
examples.trace: Step1: Store pairs: key 1→'apple', 2→'banana', 3→'cherry'. Step2: Retrieve key 2 → 'banana'. Step3: Check key 4 → not stored, so false.

examples.input: Store pairs: ('name','Alice'), ('age',30), ('city','NYC'). Retrieve 'name' and 'age'.
examples.trace: Step1: Store 'name'→'Alice', 'age'→30, 'city'→'NYC'. Step2: Retrieve 'name' → 'Alice'. Step3: Retrieve 'age' → 30.

testCases.input: Pairs: (1,10), (2,20), (3,30); get values for keys 2 and 3
Pairs: (10,100); store new pair (10,200); get value for key 10
Pairs: (3,30), (4,40); store new pair (5,50); get value for key 5
Pairs: (1,10), (2,20); check if keys 1 and 3 exist
